title: Reading a true bearing from a diagram
problem: A compass diagram has north pointing straight up. A direction is marked $125°$ clockwise from north. Write this direction as a true bearing.

steps.instruction: Recall the definition of a true bearing.
steps.working: A true bearing is the angle measured clockwise from north to the direction. It is always written as a three-figure number, e.g. $005°$, $090°$, $125°$.

steps.instruction: Read the angle from the diagram.
steps.working: The diagram shows the direction is $125°$ clockwise from north.
The true bearing is $125°$. Since it already has three figures, no leading zero is needed.

title: True bearing of an intercardinal direction
problem: State the true bearing of south-west (SW) and explain where it appears on a compass diagram.

steps.instruction: Identify the bearings of the adjacent cardinal directions.
Find the midpoint between south and west.
Describe the position on a compass diagram.
steps.working: On a diagram with north pointing up, the SW direction points down and to the left. It is $225°$ clockwise from north — halfway between the south and west arms of the compass.

answer: The true bearing of south-west is $225°$.

title: Finding a back-bearing
problem: A ship sails from port P on a true bearing of $048°$ and arrives at port Q. The captain then needs to sail directly back to P. What bearing must the captain use for the return journey?

steps.working: The bearing from B to A is always exactly $180°$ different from the bearing from A to B, because the directions are opposite. Add $180°$ if the forward bearing is less than $180°$; subtract $180°$ if it is $180°$ or more.
The bearing from P to Q is $048°$. Since $048° < 180°$, add $180°$.
PQ points north-east ($048°$ is between $000°$ and $090°$), so QP should point south-west. A bearing of $228°$ lies between $180°$ and $270°$ (the SW sector) — this is consistent.

answer: The captain must sail on a bearing of $228°$ to return to P.
